name: B.S. General Engineering -Concentration in Mechatronics

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: This is the most coherent of the concentrations: it integrates the three mechatronics domains in a well-ordered sequence -- mechanical (MECH 234 -> MECH 236 -> ME 231 -> ME 305 System Dynamics; MECH 237), electrical (ECE 231 Circuits), and computing (CS 100, MATH 337 Linear Algebra, ENGR 301 Data Science) -- and lands them in applied courses ENGR 330 (Microcontrollers/IoT), IE 455 (Robotics & PLCs), ENGR 423 (Drone Science), ENGR 424 (Robotics Science), CS 440 (Computer Vision), and the ENGR 400 design project. The main gap is the lack of a dedicated feedback-control course; ME 305 (system dynamics) and ENGR 424 (motion control) touch it but no explicit controls course completes the loop.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a dedicated feedback-control course (ECE 431 Introduction to Feedback Control Systems exists in the catalog) after ME 305 so the mechatronics loop -- sense, model, control, actuate -- is fully closed.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is deep and well-formed (deepestPrereqChain 6, e.g. MECH 234 -> MECH 236/237 -> ME 231 -> ME 305 with MATH 222) and the robotics courses gate sensibly (ENGR 424 requires a programming course). Six codes are missing (CHEM 125A, ENGR 211, ENGR 312, GER 200, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A) but these are co-req labs, prof-skills, and GER placeholders, not broken technical chains.
dimensions.suggestion: Catalog the co-req/prof-skills codes (CHEM 125A, ENGR 211/312, PHYS 111A/121A) to clear the dangling references; the technical chains themselves need no change.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives are provided in the data, only the plan of study. This is a real omission for an integrative concentration whose graduates should demonstrate multi-domain system design.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit outcomes such as 'design and integrate mechanical, electrical, and embedded subsystems' and 'implement sensing and motion control for a robotic system'.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly (creditReconcileDelta 0; 120 vs 120) with well-loaded 12-17 credit terms and a strong, transparent technical-core-to-gen-ed ratio; the mechanical/electrical/computing split is visibly balanced.
dimensions.suggestion: State the credit weighting of the mechanical vs electrical vs computing threads so students can see the mechatronics balance at a glance.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The eight-term plan is largely clean and legible with credits, but a parsing artifact leaked a stray row titled '684 B.S. General Engineering - Concentration in Quality and Reliability Engineering' into Third Year 1st Semester, which would confuse a reader. Otherwise labeling is clear.
dimensions.suggestion: Remove the stray cross-reference row ('684 ... Quality and Reliability Engineering') that bled into Third Year 1st Semester so the mechatronics plan is not mixed with another concentration's heading.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The concentration is strongly current: CS 440 (Computer Vision), ENGR 423 (Drone Science), ENGR 424 (Robotics Science), ENGR 330 (IoT/microcontrollers), ME 441 (CAE simulation), and ENGR 301 (Data Science) reflect where mechatronics and robotics practice is today.
dimensions.suggestion: Introduce a robot software-framework element (e.g. ROS) and hardware-in-the-loop simulation within ENGR 424/ME 441 so students use the toolchains industry expects.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific and technical -- ME 305 (dynamic-system modeling and simulation), ME 231 (kinematic analysis of mechanisms), CS 440 (computer-vision methods), ENGR 424 (robot sensing, motion control, motion planning) -- with no thin or placeholder text.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated unambiguously and are internally consistent, including multi-course conjunctions (ME 305 requires MATH 222, MECH 236, ME 231; ME 231 requires MATH 213 and a C in MECH 234). Only the co-req lab/prof-skills codes read as dangling.

missingTopics.rationale: ME 305 (system dynamics) and ENGR 424 (motion control) approach control, but no standalone controls course (ECE 431 exists in the catalog) closes the sense-model-control-actuate loop.
ENGR 330 (microcontrollers/IoT) is an entry point, but real-time firmware, RTOS, and detailed sensor/actuator interfacing are not developed further.
Robotics perception (CS 440) and system dynamics assume signal fundamentals that no dedicated DSP course provides.
ENGR 400 is a general multidisciplinary capstone; a mechatronics-specific integration project would consolidate the mechanical/electrical/computing threads.

emergingTrends.rationale: CS 440 (Computer Vision) and ENGR 424 (Robotics Science) position the track for ML-based perception, SLAM, and autonomous behavior, the frontier of robotics.
Adding ROS/Gazebo tooling around ENGR 424 aligns graduates with the de-facto industry robotics software stack.
Collaborative robotics and safe human-robot interaction are a fast-growing industrial segment the IE 455/ENGR 424 base could extend into.
ME 441 (CAE simulation) is a natural springboard to digital-twin and HIL workflows now standard in mechatronic development.

topSuggestions: Add a dedicated feedback-control course (ECE 431) after ME 305 to close the mechatronics control loop.
Remove the stray '684 Quality and Reliability' row that leaked into the Third Year plan.
Publish explicit multi-domain mechatronics learning outcomes.
Introduce ROS/hardware-in-the-loop tooling within ENGR 424/ME 441 for industry-standard toolchains.
Catalog the co-req/prof-skills codes (CHEM 125A, ENGR 211/312, PHYS 111A/121A) to clear dangling references.
